extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') (line 39) is passed directly to os.path.join() and then opened with open() on line 42. No sanitization or validation against '../' path traversal sequences. An attacker can read arbitrary files on the filesystem (e.g., ../../settings.py to obtain SECRET_KEY). The view even provides hints about this vulnerability (lines 44-49).

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') (line 68) is base64-decoded and passed to exec() on line 72 (and again on line 76 for Python 2 compatibility). This allows arbitrary remote code execution on the server. An attacker can execute any Python code, such as writing files, accessing databases, or spawning shells.

User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed to exec() for the Python 2 code path. Same arbitrary remote code execution vulnerability as line 72, but triggered when the first exec raises a TypeError.

The |safe filter disables Django's HTML auto-escaping for the qs variable, which comes from request.GET.get('qs') (views.py:110). The value is injected inside a <script> tag as a JavaScript string literal without JavaScript-context escaping. An attacker can break out of the string and execute arbitrary JavaScript, e.g., qs=';alert(1);//'

The |safe filter disables Django's HTML auto-escaping for the path variable, which comes from the URL path parameter (views.py:104). The value is injected inside a <script> tag as a JavaScript string literal. An attacker can craft a URL path to inject arbitrary JavaScript.

The |safe filter disables Django's HTML auto-escaping for the qs variable in an HTML attribute value (value="{{qs|safe}}"). This comes from request.GET.get('qs') (views.py:98). An attacker can break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript, e.g., qs=" onmouseover="alert(1).

The |safe filter disables Django's HTML auto-escaping for the qs variable, which comes from request.GET.get('qs') (views.py:166). The value is injected inside a <script> tag as a JavaScript string literal. An attacker can break out of the string and execute arbitrary JavaScript.

The unvalidated_redirect view takes a URL from request.GET.get('url') (line 177) and passes it directly to Django's redirect() (line 178) with no validation. This allows an attacker to craft links that redirect users to arbitrary external domains, enabling phishing attacks.

The unvalidated_forward view takes a function name from request.GET.get('fwd') (line 182), looks it up in globals() (line 183), and calls it (line 186). This allows an attacker to invoke any function in the module's global scope, including the admin() function (line 191) which renders an admin page, boom() which causes a 500 error (DoS), or code_execution() which enables RCE. This is a severe code injection vulnerability.

The missing_access_control view renders the admin page based solely on the query parameter action=admin (line 157) with no authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin interface by adding ?action=admin to the URL.

The dor_user_profile view retrieves and modifies user data based on the userid URL parameter without any authorization check. Any user can access (GET) or modify (POST) any other user's profile data, including the admin user (userid='2'), by simply changing the userid in the URL. The users dictionary on lines 115-126 contains sensitive data like admin flags.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The csrf_image view is decorated with @csrf_exempt, which disables Django's CSRF protection for this endpoint. Combined with the XSS vulnerability on the same page (qs|safe in csrf/image.html:48), an attacker can perform CSRF attacks via XSS, tricking authenticated users into performing unintended actions.

The CsrfViewMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES (line 102: #'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware'). This disables CSRF protection globally for all views, not just the explicitly exempted ones. All POST endpoints are vulnerable to CSRF attacks.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in source code: 'h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions and CSRF tokens. If the source code is accessible, an attacker can forge session cookies and CSRF tokens.

DEBUG = True is set in settings.py. In production, this exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, local variables, and settings values (including the SECRET_KEY), enabling attackers to discover sensitive information and exploit the application.

The show_toolbar callback (lines 180-185) enables the Django Debug Toolbar when 'debug' is present in GET parameters, POST data, or cookies. This exposes sensitive debugging information including SQL queries, settings, and request/response data to any user who adds ?debug=1 to any URL. Combined with DEBUG=True, this is a significant information disclosure risk.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
